summary: Jeff Atwood explores the counterintuitive idea that Internet Explorer 6's near-total market dominance (95% by 2004) actually enabled the Web 2.0 revolution rather than stifling it. He traces the history of XMLHttpRequest from its introduction as a proprietary Microsoft feature in IE5 to its adoption by Mozilla and eventual W3C standardization. The post argues that when developers didn't need to worry about cross-browser compatibility, they were free to push browser capabilities to their limits. Atwood suggests that Microsoft's abandonment of IE development, whether intentional or not, paradoxically created the conditions for rich web application development that would eventually threaten Windows applications.
key_points: XMLHttpRequest, the foundation of AJAX and Web 2.0, was a proprietary Microsoft invention introduced in IE5 in 1999
IE6 achieved unprecedented 95% market saturation by mid-2004, creating a browser monoculture
Microsoft went nearly six years without releasing a new IE version, the longest gap in browser history
The monoculture freed developers from cross-browser compatibility concerns, enabling innovation
If Microsoft intended to stifle web development by abandoning IE, the strategy backfired spectacularly
money_quotes: XMLHttpRequest is a completely proprietary feature Microsoft introduced along with IE 5.0 in March 1999
IE6 market share peaked at around 95 percent sometime in mid-2004
If 95% of the world is browsing with IE 6, pursuing browser independence is a waste of time
If you don't have to worry about browser independence, you are suddenly free to exploit advanced browser techniques like XMLHttpRequest
The super-saturation and monoculture of IE6 from 2002 to 2004 created an incredibly rich, vibrant development platform where developers were free to push the capabilities of the browser to its limits
If you subscribe to the 'evil Microsoft' theory, Microsoft would have been far better off releasing new, mildly incompatible versions of Internet Explorer every year
tone: analytical, contrarian, historically grounded with a touch of irony
key_insight: Browser monoculture, typically seen as harmful, paradoxically enabled Web 2.0 innovation by freeing developers from cross-browser compatibility burdens.
